actions.thought: 5c 9h is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Even 3-handed on the button, attempting to steal the blinds with absolute trash like 95o is usually unprofitable and unnecessary. It's better to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or better blockers to open.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 7h. The Button folded, leaving it between me and the Big Blind. While 67-offsuit is a connector, it's quite weak to play out of position. Given it's the first hand of the tournament, I'll play conservatively and fold to avoid a difficult post-flop situation against the Big Blind with a marginal hand.

In a three-handed game, the Button has a wide opening range. King-Seven offsuit is a standard hand to open-raise with to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A raise to 2.5 times the big blind is a standard sizing for this situation.

Holding Jack-seven offsuit from the small blind against a button open is a sub-optimal spot. I am out of position with a marginal hand that will rarely flop well enough to continue comfortably. Given this is the early stage of the tournament, folding is the most disciplined and +EV choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K9 suited is a solid hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. We are getting good pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 400, needing about 27% equity) and our hand flops reasonably well with flush and straight potentials, as well as decent top pair equity. Calling is standard to close the pre-flop action and see a flop.

I flopped top pair with a very strong kicker (K9) on a 9-7-2 board. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard and most profitable play is to check to the pre-flop raiser. This allows our opponent to continuation-bet with their bluffs and weaker value hands, keeping their range wide. We can comfortably evaluate and likely call if they bet, or control the pot size if they check back.

I have middle pair (sevens) with a King kicker on a 9-2-7 board with two hearts. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and my opponent checked, a continuation bet is standard to protect my hand from overcards and draws, while also getting value from weaker hands. A small bet of around 40% of the pot is effective here to deny equity to random overcards.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (K9) on a draw-heavy 9-7-2 board. Facing a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550, calling is the most standard and profitable play. A raise would likely fold out my opponent's bluffs, weaker pairs, and draws while isolating me against a stronger range like overpairs or sets. By calling, I keep their range wide and keep the pot manageable while out of position.

The Ace of hearts is a very bad card for my hand. It completes the obvious flush draw and puts an overcard to my pair of nines on the board, hitting the pre-flop raiser's range heavily. My pair of nines is now second pair. As the out of position player, checking is the standard play. Leading out (donking) makes no sense on a card that favors my opponent's range so heavily. I need to check and evaluate my opponent's action.

The Ace of hearts is a very significant card on this board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace generally favors my range more than the Big Blind's. However, I currently have middle pair (sevens) and a strong flush draw with the King of hearts. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and guarantee myself a look at the river card. This allows me to realize my equity and potentially improve to a flush or two pair, while also avoiding a potential check-raise that would force me to make a difficult decision with a hand that still has significant showdown value.

The river is a blank 8c. The opponent checked back the turn, capping their range to mostly weak one-pair hands, marginal Aces, or busted draws. My pair of Nines with a King kicker has decent showdown value, but I'm unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet. Checking allows me to see a free showdown or catch a bluff if they missed a draw.

I have a pair of sevens with a King kicker on a board where an Ace appeared on the turn. After my opponent checked for the third time, it is likely they have a marginal showdown hand or a missed draw. My pair of sevens has significant showdown value in this spot, especially since the action went check-check on the turn. Betting doesn't accomplish much as better hands are unlikely to fold, and worse hands are unlikely to call. Checking back is the safest way to realize my equity and win the pot against missed draws or lower pairs.

I am in the Button position with 3h 5h. While this is a weak suited connector, the cost to enter the pot is only 100 chips, and I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Calling here allows me to see a flop cheaply and evaluate my options.

I have pocket eights (8h 8d) in the Small Blind. The Button limped. 88 is a strong starting hand but plays better heads-up, especially out of position. I'll make a standard isolation raise to 400 to try and fold out the Big Blind, take the betting lead, and potentially win the pot outright or get heads-up against the limper.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o, a very weak hand. Flashlight limped from the Button, and Pro raised to 400 from the Small Blind. Calling 300 more would put me out of position against a potentially strong range from Pro. Q3o does not play well post-flop and the risk of being dominated or having to fold to continuation bets is too high. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

3h 5h is a weak, speculative hand. After limping into the pot, facing a significant 4x raise (400 chips) from the Small Blind, the pot odds do not justify continuing with such a marginal hand. Even with position, the cost of calling (300 more chips) is too high for the potential equity I hold. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a negative expected value situation.

Holding 75o on the button is a very weak starting hand, even in a 3-handed game. Opening with this trashy offsuit 1-gapper has a negative expected value and offers poor playability post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot to attack the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit (Td 4h). Pro has already folded from the Button. While I only have to call 50 more to see a flop, T4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. Flashlight has shown some passivity by folding to a raise earlier, but T4o doesn't justify a steal attempt here or a complete. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Queen-Nine offsuit. This is a standard opening hand for this position. A raise allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a post-flop game with positional advantage. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

Pocket Jacks are a premium hand that plays well against a button open. Being in the small blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand, so I prefer to take the betting lead and potentially narrow the field by 3-betting. This allows me to define the hand strength early and exert pressure, rather than just calling and playing a multi-way pot out of position.
Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, my hand (8-3 offsuit) is far too weak to continue. Easy fold.

I opened with a standard raise from the button with Queen-Nine offsuit, but Flashlight responded with a significant 3-bet from the small blind. While I have positional advantage, Q9o is a weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet, as it is likely dominated by Flashlight's value range (like AQ, KQ, or medium-to-high pairs) and doesn't play well enough post-flop to justify a call. Folding is the most disciplined move here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Queen-four offsuit is a weak starting hand with very little potential for post-flop equity. Playing this hand from the button is not recommended as it lacks sufficient strength to raise or call against opponents effectively. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision to preserve my stack for stronger hands in this early stage of the tournament.

It folds to me in the Small Blind. I hold 9d 3d, which is a very weak, disconnected hand. Even though it is suited, playing such a weak hand out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and conserve my stack.

With 42s on the button in a 3-handed game, the hand is too weak to play. Although we have position, opening with such a low suited one-gapper is generally not profitable as it has poor playability post-flop and little equity when called or 3-bet. Folding is the correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Q7o is a weak hand to play out of position. While it's tempting to complete the blind for 50 more, playing a marginal hand out of position against an opponent who has already shown aggression in previous hands (like in Hand #5) is generally a losing strategy in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Holding J8o on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open for a steal. I will raise to 2.5bb (250) to put pressure on the blinds. If I face significant resistance, I can easily fold, but the positional advantage justifies the open.

I hold 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. With a raise from the button, I would be forced to play the rest of the hand out of position with a dominated hand and no equity. Calling or raising here is mathematically incorrect and would significantly decrease my expected value. The only sensible strategic decision is to fold.

With Queen-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind facing a standard Button open, calling is the most profitable play. The pot odds are favorable (150 to win 400), and QJo plays reasonably well post-flop against a wide Button range. 3-betting is an option as a bluff, but flatting keeps variance low and keeps the opponent's weaker hands in the pot.

I flopped a gutshot straight draw on an A-K-K board. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, who has a massive range and nut advantage on this texture (with many strong Ax and Kx combinations), the optimal play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their continuation bet sizing.

The board (Ah Ks Kh) heavily favors my pre-flop raising range from the button. I have range advantage here. Pro checked, which is expected. I will fire a small continuation bet of 200 (approx 36% pot) to capitalize on that range advantage and force folds from his air hands. If called, I can reassess on the turn, but this board is difficult for him to continue on without at least a pair or an Ace.

I have a gutshot straight draw with Queen-Jack on an A-K-K board, but my hand has very little equity against the pre-flop raiser's range, which connects heavily with this texture. Additionally, the board is paired, meaning even if I hit my straight, I could be drawing dead to a full house. Calling is unprofitable here, so the best action is to fold.

9-8 offsuit is a weak starting hand in a three-handed game. Even with the positional advantage of the button, the hand lacks sufficient equity to justify calling the big blind or raising to steal the pot. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable opportunity.
With an unsuited one-gapper (86o) in the Small Blind against the Big Blind, the hand is too weak to play profitably out of position. The standard and optimal play is to fold.
I am dealt a very weak unsuited hand (9c 3d) on the Button. This is an easy pre-flop fold, as it has virtually no playability or equity to justify entering the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-6 offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While 8-6o has some potential in blind-vs-blind play, it is generally a weak hand to play out of position against a stack that covers me. Folding is the most disciplined choice to avoid unnecessary risks early in the tournament with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Ten-Three offsuit. While button opening ranges are wide in a three-handed game, T3o is far too weak to reliably open or call. Flashlight in the small blind has shown some aggression previously, making a steal attempt with such a low-equity hand risky. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with a strong hand, Ace-Ten suited. Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Raising is the optimal strategy to maximize the value of my strong hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 300 to apply pressure.
Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, AJo is well ahead of his wide steal range. 3-betting for value to take down the pot or play a larger pot in position is a strong, standard play here.

Pro has 3-bet my initial raise to 900. With Ace-Ten suited, I have a hand with good equity that plays well against a wide blind-vs-blind range. 4-betting out of position against a potentially strong range is high variance. Calling allows me to realize my equity in a manageable pot, while folding would be too tight given my position and hand strength.

The flop of 8s 7d 9d is highly coordinated and interacts perfectly with my hand, Ace-Ten of spades. I have the nut flush draw and an open-ended straight draw, giving me massive equity against almost any range Pro might hold. By betting here, I maintain the initiative, put significant pressure on marginal made hands, and build a pot that I have a very high likelihood of winning, even if called.

The 8s 7d 9d flop heavily connects with the Small Blind's pre-flop calling range, and their donk lead of 1000 into an 1800 pot signals significant strength, such as a made straight, two pair, set, or a strong combo draw. Holding only Ace-high, a gutshot straight draw to the Ten, and one overcard, we lack the necessary equity to continue against this aggression. Folding is the correct play.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack suited, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. Opening the pot with a raise is the standard and most profitable strategic move to take initiative and build value. A 2.5x raise (250 chips) is an appropriate size to open the betting.

86o is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the button, calling or raising from the small blind with this hand is -EV, especially out of position. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with 63o. Flashlight raised to 250 from the Button and the Small Blind folded. While the price to call is only 150 more into a pot of 400, 63o is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Defending with such a wide and weak range against a button open is generally a losing strategy. I will fold to preserve my stack.

K8s is a solid starting hand in a 3-handed game. Being on the Button, raising here gives me the chance to win the blinds immediately or play in position post-flop with the betting initiative. A raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) is standard to pressure the small and big blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-6 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x. While 7-6o has some connectivity, it is generally a fold in this position against a standard open. Calling would put me out of position for the remainder of the hand against an aggressive opponent, and 3-betting this specific hand as a bluff is less ideal than using hands with better blockers. To preserve my stack for more favorable spots, I will fold.

The opponent (Pro) is opening from the button, which is a standard steal range. With Ace-Six offsuit in the Big Blind, I have sufficient equity to defend. Calling is the standard, low-variance approach here, as it allows me to see a flop with a decent hand while keeping the pot manageable, given I am in position post-flop.

The flop [2s, Qs, 9h] does not connect with my hand [As, 6h] beyond having an Ace-high, which is likely behind a button opening range. Being out of position, checking to the aggressor is the standard and most defensive play. I do not have sufficient equity to lead out as a bluff, so I will check to see how the opponent proceeds.

The board of 2s Qs 9h connects decently well with the big blind's calling range, which contains a lot of Qx, 9x, and spade draws. With just King-high and a backdoor flush draw, checking behind is a safe play. It controls the size of the pot, avoids a check-raise that would force us to fold immediately, and allows us to realize any equity if we hit a King or pick up a heart draw on the turn.

Both players checked on the flop and the turn. As the Big Blind, I have checked, and the button (Pro) also checked. The action has checked through, so checking is the correct play as I have only Ace-high and no strong showdown value or significant draw. I will check to see the river for free.

The turn brings the Jack of hearts, giving me a king-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, which adds massive equity to my hand. Flashlight has checked twice, indicating weakness or a marginal made hand. By betting now, I can semi-bluff to fold out better high-card hands or weak pairs while building the pot for the times I complete my strong draws. A bet sizing of around 60% pot puts the right amount of pressure.

I hold the nut flush draw (As, 6h) on a board of [2s, Qs, 9h, Jh]. The opponent has bet 350 into the pot. While my direct pot odds are slightly below the mathematical requirement for a call, the strength of the nut flush draw combined with substantial implied odds makes calling the most profitable long-term decision. Folding would be too conservative, and raising as a semi-bluff is unnecessary when I can realize my equity by simply calling.

The board has completed a four-card straight (9-T-J-Q) with the Ten of Clubs on the river. My Ace-high hand has no showdown value against an opponent's range, which likely includes many Kings or Eights that now have a straight. Since I am out of position and have no strength to value bet, checking is the standard play to minimize losses and see a showdown. I do not have sufficient equity to bluff, so I will check and evaluate any further action from the opponent.

The river brings the Ten of clubs, completing a 4-card straight (9-T-J-Q) on the board. My King gives me a King-high straight, which is exceptionally strong and effectively the nuts, losing only to an unlikely AK (which would have likely 3-bet pre-flop). Since Flashlight checked to me, I have a clear value bet. Any 8 makes a lower straight (Queen-high) and is almost guaranteed to call a bet. A sizing around 70% of the pot (850 chips) puts maximum pressure on an 8x hand, two pair, or sets that might hero-call, while allowing us to extract significant value.

My Ace-high hand has no showdown value on this highly connected board, which connects well with the opponent's opening range. Any pair or made straight beats me, and calling would be a hero call without a strong bluff-catcher. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to open for a raise to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage. A standard opening size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is effective here, as it has been a consistent opening size in this game and allows me to build a pot while maintaining flexibility if I face a 3-bet from the blinds.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (8-5 offsuit). Flash has opened from the button. Calling out of position against a button raise with such a marginal hand is poor strategy as I will be playing the rest of the hand without initiative and against a range that is likely superior to mine. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Facing a 2.5x open from the Button, 74o is far too weak and disconnected to defend in the Big Blind. It lacks the equity and playability needed to continue out of position. The optimal move is a clear fold.

Holding 8-2 suited on the button is a very weak hand with low equity. Despite having position, there is no strategic reason to invest chips in this hand against the blinds. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve stack depth.

With K8o in the small blind and the button folding, this is a prime opportunity to steal the big blind. Raising to 300 applies pressure to the big blind and will frequently win the pot uncontested pre-flop. If called, we still hold a strong King.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 2c. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. To call, I need to put in 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While I will have post-flop position against the Small Blind, 32s is one of the weakest suited hands and realization of equity is difficult. I've observed Pro being active and aggressive in previous hands. At this stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot rather than defending with the very bottom of my range.

Q3s is a very weak hand, even from the Button. While stealing the blinds is an option, it is easily dominated by better hands that the blinds might call or 3-bet with. Folding is the standard and safest play.

Holding King-Eight offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folds is a standard raising situation. K8o is well ahead of the average range of the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to either take the pot down immediately or play a pot against the Big Blind with a superior hand.

I am in the big blind with 5h 7c, which is a very weak hand. Even though the pot odds make the call enticing (200 to win 500), playing this hand out of position post-flop against an aggressive player like Flash is a losing proposition. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand to contest the pot.

I am on the button with 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the button is a good position to attempt a steal, 6-2o is too weak to justify a raise given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with 7d 9d. The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind (Pro). Since 7d 9d is a respectable suited connector, I will complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply and maintain a manageable pot size, avoiding an unnecessary raise that could lead to a tough spot out of position.

With Q5o in the Big Blind facing a limp from the Small Blind, I have a weak hand that doesn't play well in a bloated pot. Checking my option to see a free flop in position is the standard and most profitable play here.

The flop is 5d-4h-Jc, and my hand (7d-9d) has missed completely, providing only a weak backdoor flush draw. As the first to act in this limped pot, betting offers little value against an opponent's likely range, which often includes a Jack or a piece of the board. Checking is the most prudent strategy to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the turn card without unnecessary risk.

I hit middle pair on a relatively dry flop. Flashlight checked to me, which often indicates weakness such as missed overcards. Betting here helps deny equity to those overcards and can get value from weaker holdings like Ace-high or gutshots. A half-pot bet is a good size to accomplish this without over-committing chips with a marginal made hand.

I am in the small blind with 7d 9d on a flop of 5d-4h-Jc. The board is somewhat coordinated, but I have no made hand and only a backdoor flush draw. My opponent (Pro) has bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. With 7-high and no strong draw, I lack the equity to continue profitably against a bet. Out of position, continuing in this hand is a mistake, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am holding pocket Kings, which is a premium hand and a strong favorite against almost any range. Being on the button, I want to take the initiative, build the pot, and narrow the field. A raise to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard sizing to accomplish these goals, setting up a good stack-to-pot ratio while capitalizing on my position.

I am dealt 2s7h, which is structurally the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 from the Button while in the Small Blind, folding is the only profitable long-term decision. I have no equity, terrible playability, and positional disadvantage.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine suited, a very strong hand for defending against a standard Button open. The pot odds are favorable, as I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650. This hand has excellent playability and can flop strong draws or top pairs. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity post-flop against what is likely a wide Button range.

On a flop of 6d-Kh-6c, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture favors the Button's range, as they are more likely to have Kings or pocket pairs. My Ten-high has no direct draws and very little equity. The standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action. I intend to fold to most bet sizes as this board does not connect with my hand at all.

I have flopped the top full house with pocket Kings on a board of 6d-Kh-6c. This is essentially the nuts at this point. Since my opponent checked to me, I need to start building the pot immediately to extract value from any Aces, lower pocket pairs, or speculative hands they might have. A bet of 350, which is roughly 50% of the pot, is appropriate to encourage a call without being too intimidating.

On a flop of 6d-Kh-6c, I have Ten-high with no draws or meaningful equity. Flashlight's continuation bet of 350 into a 650 pot is standard on this board texture, which favors the pre-flop raiser's range (specifically King-x and high pocket pairs). While I defended the Big Blind with a playable hand, this specific board does not connect with my Ten-Nine of spades at all. I have no backdoor flush draw and only the weakest of backdoor straight possibilities. Continuing by calling or raising here would be a low-frequency, high-risk play with little chance of success against a range that contains many stronger hands. The most prudent action is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.
Qh2s is a very weak offsuit hand. Being first to act as the Button, the correct play is to fold and save chips for better opportunities.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a standard hand to open-raise with to either take the blinds immediately or play the pot with initiative against the big blind. I will raise to 300 total.

Facing a raise from the small blind while in the big blind, defending with K4s is a standard play given the pot odds and the fact that I will have position post-flop. Calling the 200 to match the 300 raise allows me to see a flop with a decent hand and keep my range wide, as the opponent has shown aggressive tendencies.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have an Ace-high hand on a paired board (8-6-6). This board is generally favorable for the raiser's range, although the Big Blind can occasionally have a 6. I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) to take the initiative and potentially end the hand now if the opponent missed the flop.

The board (8s, 6h, 6s) is paired, and I hold only K-high with no meaningful equity or draw. My opponent showed strength by raising pre-flop and leading out on the flop. Calling here is mathematically incorrect and against a range that likely connects with this board texture. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with pocket sevens, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise to 300 to take control of the pot and build value.

Holding Jack-Deuce offsuit in the small blind against a button raise is extremely weak. My equity is poor, and I am out of position. Calling would be a losing play in the long run. I should fold and conserve my stack for a more profitable situation.
Jc 3c is a very weak starting hand, even suited. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, defending with such a wide gap and weak kicker out of position is unprofitable. Folding is the correct move.

Pocket Tens are a strong hand that deserves to be played aggressively. As the dealer button, I have the advantage of acting last throughout the hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that builds the pot and helps define my range while putting pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with A2o. The Button raised to 3x (300). A2o is a weak offsuit Ace, which plays poorly out of position post-flop and doesn't do well enough to call. A 3-bet bluff is an option but generally not worth the risk here, so folding is the standard and most optimal choice.

I am in the Big Blind with 53o, which is a very weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. Flashlight has raised to 3x from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, the hand is difficult to play out of position and does not flop well. Fold is the most disciplined and standard play here.
67o is too weak of a hand to open even from the Button, as it lacks both high card value and suitedness. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Ten-Trey is a very weak starting hand with poor playability post-flop. While I only need to commit 50 more to see a flop, this hand rarely wins at showdown and is difficult to bluff with out of position. Given that we are early in the tournament (Hand 22 of 150) and I have a healthy stack of 94 big blinds, there is no reason to play such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

8-4 offsuit is a very weak hand, even from the button in a 3-handed game. While the button offers a good opportunity to steal the blinds, this specific hand has poor playability post-flop if called. I've been active in previous hands, so I don't need to force a play here. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening hand.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (4d 2s). Given that my opponent is in the big blind and I am out of position, playing this hand is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with a strong suited Ace (Ac5c) in a three-handed game. This is a very favorable position and a premium hand for short-handed play. Raising to 300 allows me to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop, or at least gain the initiative to play the rest of the hand with the positional advantage.
I hold 72o, the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, folding from the Small Blind is the only reasonable and mathematically sound decision.

I have been dealt pocket Queens, a premium starting hand. Flashlight opened from the button with a raise to 300. With such a strong hand, I want to build the pot and take the initiative. A 3-bet is standard here to extract value and potentially narrow the field or win the pot immediately. I will raise to 900, which is approximately three times the initial raise size.

I am on the button with Ace-Five suited, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My opponent, Flash, has 3-bet from the big blind. While A5s is a strong hand, 4-betting against an aggressive player from the big blind can be risky and bloat the pot unnecessarily. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop to evaluate the board. Given my stack size and the pot odds, calling is the most balanced and profitable approach.

I have flopped top set (Queens) on a very dry board of 7s-Qh-3c. Pre-flop, I 3-bet and Flashlight called. Since the board is so disconnected, there are very few draws I need to worry about. My goal is to extract maximum value. Betting small here (around 1/3 pot) serves two purposes: it allows Flashlight to stay in the hand with pocket pairs (JJ, TT, 99) or Ace-high hands, and it looks like a standard continuation bet. Checking is an option to trap, but building the pot now ensures I can play for stacks on later streets.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. The opponent, Flash, is aggressive and is likely c-betting a range that includes many bluffs here. With position, calling is the most efficient way to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable. I can re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and the opponent's subsequent action.

I have flopped top set with pocket Queens and the turn Ten of Diamonds is a relatively safe card. While it does complete a potential J9 straight, that hand is unlikely for Flashlight to have called a 3-bet with pre-flop. Being on the button with Q-T offsuit, this is a solid hand to open in a three-handed game. A standard raise to 300 is an appropriate sizing to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

9-5 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially out of position. Investing more chips into the pot with such a weak holding is not strategically sound. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 9-5 suited. This is a weak, marginal hand that does not warrant an open-raise or a call in this position. Given my conservative strategy to maintain my stack in this 3-handed game, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the weakness of my hand and being out of position against the Big Blind post-flop, the mathematically sound and standard decision is to fold, saving my remaining chips for better opportunities.

Playing 3-handed, I am on the button with 5d6d, a strong suited connector. Opening with a raise allows me to potentially steal the blinds or take control of the hand in position if called. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. The button has opened with a standard raise to 250. Ace-Eight is typically ahead of a button's wide opening range. While calling is an option, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, and avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position if the Big Blind calls. I will raise to 850 to put pressure on the Button and define my hand strength.

I have been dealt Ace-King offsuit in the big blind, which is a premium holding. With Pro opening the pot and Flash 3-betting, I am facing significant aggression. Rather than flat calling and potentially playing a multi-way pot out of position, I will 4-bet to take the initiative and isolate against the aggressors. Raising to 2200 is a standard size to put pressure on my opponents and build a pot that I have a significant equity advantage in.

I opened with a suited connector on the button, but faced a 3-bet from the Small Blind and a cold 4-bet from the Big Blind. A cold 4-bet from the Big Blind indicates extreme strength, likely premium pairs or AK. My 5d6d is entirely dominated by their ranges, and calling would commit nearly a third of my stack with a highly speculative hand. Folding is the only mathematically sound decision.

I 3-bet A8o from the Small Blind to 850 against Pro's button open to take control of the hand. However, Flashlight in the Big Blind has now cold 4-bet to 2200. This is a very strong action that usually represents a premium range like JJ+, AK, or AQ. A8o is significantly behind this range and does not play well out of position in a 4-bet pot. Even though I have already committed 850 chips, calling another 1350 is likely a losing play in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. While I have been aggressive in opening from the button in previous hands (like Hand 61 with 9-6 suited), 9-6 offsuit is significantly weaker and at the bottom of a 3-handed opening range. Given that Flashlight (the chip leader) has shown aggression and Pro (the short stack) may be looking for opportunities to defend or jam, I will choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to maintain my stack.

I am in the small blind with 8s5s after the button folded. The big blind (Pro) is in the hand. Completing the small blind to see a flop with suited connectors is a reasonable strategy here to play post-flop, given the pot odds and my stack size as the chip leader.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4h, which is a very weak hand. The Small Blind limped in, giving me the option to check and see a free flop. There is no reason to bloat the pot or attempt a bluff with such poor equity pre-flop, so I will happily check.

I am in the small blind with 8s5s and the board is 4d 4s Tc. I have missed this flop completely and have no significant equity. Since I am out of position against the Big Blind (Pro), checking is the most prudent strategy to keep the pot size controlled and see if I can catch a better turn card or give up if the opponent shows strength.

We flopped trip fours in a limped pot against the Small Blind. Flashlight checked to us. With such a strong hand, we want to start building the pot and extracting value from a Ten, smaller pocket pairs, or stubborn floats. A half-pot bet of 100 is small enough to keep their range wide while starting to get money in.

I have absolutely no equity on this board with 8-high (8s 5s) on a paired board (4d 4s Tc). There is no draw to chase, and my hand is essentially air. Even though the pot odds are tempting, calling with 8-high when the opponent shows interest in the pot is a losing play in the long run. Folding is the only disciplined option.

I am dealt pocket Aces, the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am on the button with a strong stack. I need to raise to build the pot and thin the field, ideally getting one or both opponents to commit chips while I have the advantage of position.
Jack-Deuce offsuit is a very weak, unplayable hand. Facing a raise from the Button while in the Small Blind, the correct and only viable play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Eight of Spades (T8o). Flashlight (the Button) has raised to 300, and Pro (Small Blind) has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, giving me pot odds of 3.25 to 1. While T8o is a mediocre hand and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, these odds are generally sufficient to defend the Big Blind against a standard button open from an active player. I'll call and see how the flop develops.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) on a flop of 9d-Qh-6h after defending my big blind with T8o against a button raise. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to see how they react to the board. I am out of position and want to see if I can realize my equity cheaply or potentially use my draw as a semi-bluff if the opportunity arises later.

I have pocket Aces on a relatively dry, rainbow flop (9d-Qh-6h). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and my opponent checked, a continuation bet is the standard play to extract value from weaker hands like a Queen, middle pairs, or potential straight draws. I want to build the pot while the board is still safe.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a board of 9d-Qh-6h. Flashlight has fired a continuation bet of 400 into the 650 pot. With 8 outs to improve to a straight (any 7 or any Jack), I have approximately 32% equity to hit by the river. The pot odds are roughly 3.6 to 1, making this a mathematically sound call to see the turn. While there is a heart flush draw on the board, my primary goal is to realize my equity. I will call and evaluate the turn, keeping the pot manageable while playing out of position.

On the turn, the Seven of Hearts completes my open-ended straight draw, giving me a 6-7-8-9-10 straight. However, this is a highly coordinated and dangerous board. The 7h also completes a possible heart flush, and any hand containing a Jack (like J8 or JT) would make a higher straight than mine. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the most prudent play is to check. This allows me to control the pot size and see how Flashlight reacts to the flush and straight possibilities. If he checks behind, I can safely see the river; if he bets, I will likely call to see the final card, though I must be cautious of a flush.

The turn card 7h is dangerous, completing potential flush and straight draws on a board that already featured a Queen. My pocket Aces are now effectively a bluff-catcher against any made hand. Checking behind is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and realize my equity without overcommitting in a potentially losing situation.

On the river, the board has paired (6d) and already contains three hearts (Qh, 6h, 7h). While I have a straight (6-7-8-9-T), it is the low end of possible straights (a Jack-Ten makes a higher straight) and loses to any flush or full house. Flashlight's check on the turn suggests they might be wary of the board or holding a hand with some showdown value but not enough to bet into a coordinated texture. Leading out now is risky as I'm likely only called by hands that beat me (flushes, boats, or higher straights) and I might fold out worse hands that I could otherwise beat at showdown. I will check to see if Flashlight checks behind or to evaluate a potential bet.

I have pocket Aces on a board of 9d-Qh-6h-7h-6d. The river did not improve my hand, but it also did not complete the obvious flush draw, though the straight draw 8-5 or 8-10 is possible. My opponent checked to me on the river after checking the turn, which indicates they likely have a marginal hand (like a weak Queen, a Nine, or a missed draw) or are playing trap. A bet here is unlikely to get called by worse hands frequently enough to justify the risk of being check-raised or value-cutting myself against a Six. Checking behind is the best way to realize my showdown equity and avoid losing extra chips if I am behind.
K9o is a marginal but acceptable hand to open with from the Button in a 3-handed game. I will make a standard raise to 2.5x to either steal the blinds or play a raised pot in position.

In a 3-handed game, A7o is a relatively strong hand, but being in the small blind is a difficult position. Pro's button raise of 250 is standard. To avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position or allowing the big blind a cheap entry, I will 3-bet to 850. This exerts pressure on the button's wide opening range and allows me to take control of the pot.

I am in the big blind with King-Queen offsuit. The button opened the betting, and the small blind 3-bet, which represents a strong or polarized range. KQ offsuit is a strong hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, but it is not strong enough to profitably 4-bet against two opponents, especially considering the potential for a 4-bet to be met with a shove. Calling allows me to realize my equity in a significant pot with favorable stack depth. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds.
K9o is too weak to continue against a 3-bet from the Small Blind and a cold call from the Big Blind. This indicates significant strength from both opponents, making a fold the only viable option.

As the pre-flop 3-better, I represent a very strong range including premium pairs (JJ-AA) and big broadway cards. The flop of T-6-4 rainbow is relatively disconnected and dry, making it a good candidate for a continuation bet. Flashlight's cold-call of my 3-bet from the Big Blind is a strong signal, likely indicating a medium pocket pair (77-JJ) or suited broadways like AQ/AJ. By betting 700 (approximately 36% of the pot), I maintain my aggressive posture and put pressure on his overcards and marginal pairs. If he calls, I have some showdown value with Ace-high and can evaluate the turn card. If he raises, I can safely fold knowing my Ace-high is likely beaten.

Flash is continuing with a bet of 700 into a pot of 1950. With King-Queen offsuit, I have two overcards on this 6-10-4 rainbow board. My hand has reasonable equity against the opponent's 3-betting range, and the pot odds are attractive (calling 700 to win a total pot of 2650). Folding to a single continuation bet with two overcards would be too exploitable and tight. I will call to see a turn card, as I am not ready to concede the hand yet and the stack depths allow for further play.

On the turn 4h, the board pairs the low card. My Ace-high (Ac7s) has not improved and has limited showdown value against Flashlight's calling range. Flashlight cold-called a 3-bet pre-flop and then called a continuation bet on a T-6-4 flop, which strongly suggests a range of medium pocket pairs (77-99), a Ten (AT, KT, JT), or possibly JJ. A second barrel is unlikely to force these hands to fold on this board. By checking, I control the pot size and see if Flashlight checks back with a hand I might still beat or a draw, or if he bets, which would likely signify a hand stronger than my Ace-high.

I am in the big blind with King-Queen high on a board of 6d-Th-4c-4h. My opponent (Flash) checked to me on the turn. I have no pair and no realistic draw. Betting here would be turning a hand with minimal showdown value into a bluff, which is likely ineffective against an opponent who has already shown aggression by 3-betting pre-flop and betting the flop. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, potentially realize my equity if I hit a King or Queen on the river, and keeps the pot manageable if my opponent is trapping or has a marginal made hand.

The board has run out 6d-Th-4c-4h-5h. My hand is Ace-high (Ac7s), which has very little showdown value against Flashlight's range. Flashlight cold-called my 3-bet pre-flop and called a continuation bet on the flop, which strongly suggests a range of medium pocket pairs (77-99) or a Ten (Tx). His check on the turn indicates he is likely looking for a cheap showdown with one of those marginal hands. At this point, a bluff is unlikely to succeed against a Ten or a pocket pair that has committed this much to the pot, especially after the turn was checked through. Checking is the most prudent play to see if my Ace-high can win against missed broadway cards or to minimize losses if I am beaten.

I hold King-Queen high on a board of 6d-Th-4c-4h-5h. With no pair and no straight or flush potential, my hand has zero showdown value. Flash showed initial aggression pre-flop and on the flop, but checked the turn and river, suggesting they may have given up or have a marginal hand. A bet here would be a pure bluff against a range that likely has me beaten, and I have no reason to try and represent a hand I do not have. Checking is the most prudent action to conclude the hand without risking further chips.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit (J5o). This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well in multi-way pots or post-flop. While the button offers a positional advantage, J5o is far outside a standard opening range even for an aggressive strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.
Holding 5h 4c in the small blind is a weak hand. With poor potential and being out of position against the big blind, folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine offsuit. While I have position, this is a relatively weak hand, especially against two opponents. Given the current tournament situation and the strength of the hand, I prefer to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable opportunity rather than playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop if called.

With Q9o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a decent starting hand to attempt a steal against the Big Blind. Raising puts pressure on Flash and gives me the initiative, which will be useful if the pot goes to the flop. A raise to 3 BB is standard here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 2d. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind after the Button folded. While I have position post-flop and the pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), 62s is an extremely weak hand that rarely realizes its equity well. Pro has been playing a relatively solid game, and defending with such a low-ranking suited hand out of the Big Blind is generally a losing play in the long run, even against a wide Small Blind opening range. I will fold and wait for a more profitable situation.

I am dealt 42o on the Button. This is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em and falls well outside of any acceptable opening range. I will simply fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The button has folded, making this a blind-vs-blind situation. A7o is a strong hand in this spot, significantly ahead of a random Big Blind range. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind with K7s, a hand that has good potential against a small blind opening raise. The pot is 400, and I need to call 200 to see a flop. Given that I have position post-flop and the price is right, calling is the most balanced and strategic decision here.

I have flopped middle pair with an Ace kicker on a board of 10-7-2. This is a strong position in a blind-vs-blind battle. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to extract value from draws (like clubs) or weaker pairs and to protect my hand against overcards.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board of 7c, Tc, 2s. With a pot of 600 and a bet of 300, I am getting 3:1 pot odds, which makes calling mathematically sound. Folding would be too weak given my pair strength, and raising could force out hands I am currently beating or get me in trouble against an overpair or a stronger Ten. Calling allows me to control the pot size and evaluate the turn.

The turn completes a potential flush with the Three of Clubs on the board. My middle pair (sevens) is still decent in a blind-vs-blind battle, but the board texture is becoming increasingly dangerous. I will check to control the pot size and avoid getting raised off my hand. This allows me to see the river for free or evaluate the strength of Flashlight's hand if they decide to bet.

The board has three clubs, and Flash checked to me, indicating either weakness or a trap. With a pair of sevens and no club in my hand, I do not have the nuts or a strong draw. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as most hands I beat would fold, and hands that call likely have me beat or have significant equity against me. Checking behind is the safest play to control the pot size and get to a cheap showdown.

On the river, the board is 7c-Tc-2s-5c-Qd. I have middle pair (sevens) with an Ace kicker. The turn 5c completed the flush draw, and the river Qd is an overcard to my pair. After I checked the turn and Flashlight checked behind, it's unlikely Flashlight has a flush or a very strong hand, as they likely would have bet for value. However, my hand isn't strong enough to bet for value here, as I'm mostly only getting called by hands that beat me (like a Ten or a Queen). Checking is the best play to reach showdown and catch potential bluffs or win against weaker pairs/missed straight draws.

Flash checked the river. I have a pair of sevens with a King kicker on a board with three clubs and a Queen. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, as most hands I beat would fold. Checking behind is the most strategic play to take the hand to showdown without risking any additional chips.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (8d 8c), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Standard play is to open with a raise to put pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a hand that likely has high equity. I will raise to 300, consistent with my previous opening sizes.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Ten suited, which is a strong hand for three-handed play. The button has opened, and I want to take the initiative and potentially isolate them before the big blind acts. A three-bet to 950 is a standard, aggressive move that puts pressure on the button and allows me to define my range or potentially take down the pot pre-flop.
K6o is a weak hand, especially facing a button open and a small blind 3-bet. Folding is the only viable option here to preserve my stack.

I am holding a strong pocket pair (8d 8c) on the button. I originally raised to 300, and Flashlight (the small blind) has now 3-bet to 950. With a stack of 11500, I have 115 big blinds, which gives me plenty of room to play. 88 is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game, but 3-bets from the blinds can represent a polar range or high-equity hands like high pairs or AK/AQ. Since I have position for the rest of the hand and a hand that plays well post-flop, calling is a solid option to see a flop without bloating the pot further against a potentially stronger range. However, fold/raise are also options. Folding 88 here is too tight. Re-raising (4-betting) might force out hands I'm ahead of or get me into trouble against AA/KK/QQ/JJ. Calling allows me to keep their bluffs in and use my positional advantage.

I am out of position after three-betting pre-flop, but I still hold the initiative. The flop is relatively dry, and with the Ace of diamonds, I have a strong blocker and high card potential. A continuation bet is standard here to maintain pressure and represent a strong range that includes overpairs. I will bet 800 to keep the pressure on my opponent and potentially win the pot now, while also setting up for future streets if called.

On a flop of 9h 6s 3s, I am holding 8d 8c. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading with a continuation bet of 800 into a pot of 1900. My hand is a mid-pocket pair, currently an overpair to two of the cards on the board but behind the 9. Flashlight's 3-bet range includes big pairs (AA-TT), which have me crushed, and big cards like AK, AQ, or AJ. The board texture is relatively safe, though there is a spade draw. I am getting roughly 3.4-to-1 odds to call. Raising here would likely only get called by hands that beat me (9x or JJ+) and force out bluffs. Calling allows me to see a turn in position, keeping their range wide and allowing me to re-evaluate if an overcard or a spade arrives. Given the stack depths and the pot odds, calling is the most prudent play.

The board has become significantly more coordinated with the arrival of the 5 of spades, completing a potential flush draw. As the out-of-position player with only Ace-high and no spade in my hand, continuing to bet into this board texture is risky. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the opponent's intentions, while also giving me the option to fold to a significant bet if I believe they have hit their flush or a made hand.

The turn card (5s) is quite dangerous as it completes a possible flush and several straight draws. My pocket eights are now essentially a mid-strength hand; I'm ahead of overcards like AK or AQ that don't have a spade, but I'm behind any 9, any overpair (TT-AA), and any completed flush. When Flashlight checks, they might be pot-controlling a marginal hand, trapping with a flush, or giving up with air. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid getting check-raised off my hand, and can evaluate the river with the intention of reaching a relatively cheap showdown.

I am sitting in the small blind with Ace-high (Ace-Ten) on a board that shows 9-6-3-5-J, with three spades present. After checking the turn and seeing my opponent also check, the river has not improved my hand. Since I do not have a spade, I cannot represent a flush, and betting here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any hand that has showdown value. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown cheaply and potentially catch a bluff from a busted draw, or simply accept that my Ace-high is likely behind any pair or better that my opponent may have.

On the river board of 9h 6s 3s 5s Jc, I hold pocket eights. The action checked through on the turn after a spade completed a potential flush. Flashlight, who 3-bet pre-flop and lead the flop, has now checked the river as well. My pocket eights have decent showdown value against missed overcards like AK or AQ that did not have a spade. However, my hand is not strong enough to bet for value, as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands and might be check-raised by a trapped flush or a better pair (9x, TT, or a slow-played JJ+). Checking back allows me to reach showdown and realize my equity without risking more chips.

K5 offsuit is a weak hand that does not warrant an opening raise or a call from the button, especially considering the current tournament stage and the strength of my opponents. Folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve my stack.
K7o is a reasonable hand to steal the big blind when it folds to me in the small blind. I will make a standard raise to 300 to pressure the big blind and try to take down the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. After the Button folds, the Small Blind (Pro) raises to 300. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, Ace-Six is a strong starting hand, typically ahead of a wide Small Blind opening range. Additionally, I have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. Calling is a standard and effective play here, as it allows me to see a flop while maintaining control over the pot size and utilizing my positional advantage post-flop.

I raised pre-flop to steal the blind, but the big blind called. The flop comes A-9-8 with a flush draw, which connects heavily with a typical big blind defending range, including pairs, straight draws, and flush draws. With just King-high and zero draws, I have virtually no equity. A continuation bet on this board texture with complete air is unlikely to get enough folds, so checking and preparing to give up the hand is the safest and most optimal play.

I am in position with top pair, weak kicker (Ace-Six) on an A-8-9 board. Pro (Small Blind) checked to me after raising pre-flop. While I have top pair, the board is fairly coordinated with straight and flush draws. Pro's check could be a trap with a stronger Ace or a medium pocket pair that is giving up. I will bet for value and protection, to charge draws and extract value from worse hands like a 9, an 8, or middle pairs. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to define where I stand.

With King-high and no draws on an A-9-8 board with a flush draw, my hand has virtually no equity. The opponent's bet of 300 indicates they likely connected with this board or are taking a stab. I have no reason to continue, so folding is the only correct decision.

With Q5s on the Button, I have a suited Queen but a very weak kicker. While it could be a marginal open in some 3-handed games, we're up against two larger stacks who have been relatively active. Folding here avoids getting involved in difficult spots post-flop with a dominated hand, preserving our 65 BB stack for better opportunities.

I have Jack-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind. Since the Button has folded, it is a head-up situation against the Big Blind. JTo is a strong starting hand in this context. I will raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the hand and build the pot, or potentially win it immediately.

With Queen-Ten offsuit in the big blind, I have favorable pot odds of 2:1 to call a raise from the small blind. Calling is the standard defensive play here to see a flop and evaluate the situation, as the hand has reasonable equity against a wide small blind opening range.

I have top pair with a Jack and a Ten kicker on a relatively dry board of 4s-8d-Jh. This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. I will lead out with a bet of 300, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker hands like middle pairs or straight draws, and to protect my equity.

I have queen-high on a board of jack, eight, four. With no pair and no draw, my equity against the opponent's opening range is very low. Calling a bet here would be speculative and likely unprofitable. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my chip stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 8d 6c, which is a weak hand. However, the pot only costs me 50 more chips to complete a call if I wanted to play, but standard strategy dictates that from the button I should either raise to put pressure on the blinds or fold. Given the stack sizes and the fact that 86o is significantly below a standard opening range even for a button steal, I will fold to maintain my stack and wait for a better spot. Both Flashlight and Pro have shown they can be aggressive or sticky, and this hand lacks post-flop playability.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. While Q9o is a marginal hand, completing the small blind (calling the additional 50 chips) is a standard play to see a flop in a heads-up situation, provided the opponent doesn't raise excessively. Given the current stack size and the small commitment, I will complete to see the flop.

We are in the Big Blind with Q5o and the Small Blind has simply completed. Q5o is a very weak hand that does not warrant raising to build a pot, but since we are already in the Big Blind and face no raise, we can check to see a free flop in position.

The flop comes K-K-8, which is a very dry board. My hand (Q-9) has not connected with the board, and as the small blind, I am first to act. Betting here would be a pure bluff against a player who checked pre-flop, and given the paired King board, it is unlikely the Big Blind folds to a small bet if they have anything at all. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

The opponent limped from the Small Blind and checked this dry, paired K-K-8 flop. Since I have air (Queen-high) but am in position, a half-pot stab here will frequently win the pot uncontested against their weak limping range.

The board is K-K-8, which is very static and heavily favors any range that includes a King or even a mid-pair. With Q-high and no real draws, my hand has minimal equity against Pro's betting range. Calling a bet of 100 into a 200-chip pot with such weak holdings is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent action in this spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I should take the initiative by raising to 300 to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot with the advantage of position.

Holding pocket sevens in the Small Blind against a Button open. This is a standard spot to flat call and set-mine, given the stack depth (over 60 BBs). A 3-bet is an option, but out of position 77 can be tricky to play post-flop if called, so I prefer keeping the pot smaller and seeing a flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9h 4d. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind (Pro) called. I am getting 3.5:1 on a call (200 to win a total pot of 700). However, 94 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially in a multi-way pot. While the price is tempting, I am out of position against both players for the rest of the hand and likely dominated by their ranges. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my healthy stack.

I flopped an underpair (pocket sevens) to the top two cards on a relatively dry Tc-8d-2s board. Acting first out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most balanced play is to check to the raiser. Donking here is sub-optimal because the opponent's range has many stronger hands like overpairs, top pair, or second pair. Checking allows me to see their c-bet sizing and decide whether to call or fold, or potentially see a free turn card if they check back.

I am in position on a relatively dry board of T-8-2 rainbow. My Ace-Jack holds overcard equity and I was the pre-flop aggressor. A continuation bet of 350 (half the pot) is a standard move to test the opponent's range, potentially forcing a fold from weaker hands that missed the flop or charging draws if they have them.

With pocket sevens on a Tc-8d-2s board, I hold third pair. While Flashlight's half-pot continuation bet from the Button could easily be a bluff or overcards, playing this hand out of position on future streets will be very difficult. Unless I hit a two-outer on the turn, I will likely face immense pressure on most runouts. My Small Blind calling range includes many stronger hands that can defend here much better, such as Tx, 8x, 99, or straight draws. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for more profitable, higher-equity situations.

7h 3h is a very weak, disconnected hand. Even though I am on the button, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a pre-flop raise or call, especially given my stack size relative to the opponents. The best action is to simply fold.

Calling the 150 allows me to keep his range wide, exercise pot control, and evaluate the turn, knowing that several turn cards could kill my action or put me far behind.

The board now shows an Ace, which is a strong card that hits the big blind's calling range often, but also serves as an excellent bluffing opportunity. Calling is the best option to keep his bluffs and weaker value hands in the pot while controlling the size of the pot in case I am already behind.

I have hit a straight (5-6-7-8-9) on the river, giving me a very strong hand. While there is a flush possibility with three spades on the board, my straight is still highly valuable. I should extract value from my opponent, who likely has an Ace or two-pair, by placing a substantial bet. A bet of 600 in a 1000 pot is a solid value bet that should get called while still protecting against potential flushes by not over-committing.

The river brings the 5s, putting three spades on the board and completing any potential backdoor flush draws. Flashlight has now fired a substantial third barrel of 600 into a 1000 pot. Flashlight is very unlikely to triple-barrel a worse value hand like two pair or a set on this highly coordinated, flush-completing board. Therefore, my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher against a very strong perceived range. Given the poor relative hand strength and the threatening board runout, folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving decision.
