I have flopped a King-high flush draw on a Jack-high board. Flashlight's continuation bet of 350 into a 650-chip pot gives me roughly 2.8:1 odds. My direct equity to hit a flush by the river is approximately 35%, and I may also have overcard outs with the King. Calling is the most appropriate action here to realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades appears, which is an overcard to the previous Jack-high board. I am still holding a King-high flush draw. In the Big Blind and out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I want to see a free river if possible or evaluate the pot odds if Flashlight decides to bet again. Leading here doesn't accomplish much against a range that likely contains many Jacks and now potentially Queens.

I have improved to top pair with a Queen on the turn. My opponent checked to me, which is consistent with them holding a draw or a weak hand. I will bet 700 into the 1300 pot to extract value from their weaker holdings and apply pressure on any flush or straight draws on the board.

I am holding a King-high flush draw on a board of 3d-Jd-6c-Qs. On the turn, the Queen of Spades is an overcard to the Jack. Flashlight has bet 700 into a 1350 pot, offering me pot odds of approximately 2.9:1. I have 9 outs to hit the flush on the river, which is roughly 19.5% equity. While the direct pot odds are slightly below the required equity (approx 25.4%), the implied odds are significant; if I hit my flush on the river, I can likely extract enough value from Flashlight's likely range of Jacks, Queens, or two-pair to make the call mathematically profitable. I will call to attempt to realize this equity.

On the river, the board is 3d-Jd-6c-Qs-8s. My King-high flush draw has missed, and I am left with King-high. I am out of position against Flashlight, who raised pre-flop and bet both the flop and the turn. In this situation, leading out with a bet is unlikely to fold out a Jack or a Queen, which are heavily represented in Flashlight's range. Checking is the most prudent play. If Flashlight checks behind, I hope to occasionally win against a missed straight draw or a lower missed flush draw, though I expect to lose the majority of the time. If Flashlight bets, I will be forced to fold as I have no showdown value.

I have two pair (Queens and Eights) on a board that does not offer an obvious flush completion, although T9 is a potential straight. My opponent has called bets on the flop and turn, indicating a range that could include draws, middle pairs, or potentially a straight. Given the strength of my hand relative to the board, a thin value bet is risky against a check-raise, and I likely won't get called by worse hands frequently enough to justify a large bet. Checking behind is the optimal play to realize my showdown value safely.

With pocket deuces on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a standard opening hand. I'll raise to 300 to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially steal the pot, while still giving myself the chance to flop a set if called.

I am holding 4d 2h in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Pro has raised from the Button to 300. Even in a three-handed game, 42o is not a hand worth defending, especially out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Nine suited, which is a solid hand to defend against a button open. The pot is offering good odds to call (200 to win 450), and being in position post-flop as the big blind defending is a standard play. Raising is an option, but calling maintains a manageable pot size and allows me to see a flop with a decent hand.

I am in the big blind out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) on a board of Qc-8s-Kd. I have Ace-high, which is essentially a bluff-catcher or a drawing hand that needs to improve. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent acts, as betting into the aggressor with just Ace-high on a board that likely hits their range is not optimal.

The flop features two broadway cards, a King and a Queen, which connect very well with my opponent's Big Blind defending range. My pocket deuces have very little equity here and are virtually drawing to a two-outer. A continuation bet is unlikely to fold out hands that beat me, such as Kx, Qx, or even middle pairs. Checking behind is the most prudent play to keep the pot small and avoid wasting chips when I am likely behind.

I am on the turn with a gutshot straight draw and a flush draw on a board of Qc-8s-Kd-Ts. The pot checked through on the flop, which often indicates capped ranges or marginal holdings from the button. Checking allows me to realize my equity and see a free river card, or potentially set up a check-raise if the opponent bluffs. Betting here as a semi-bluff against a checked-back range is risky when I have significant showdown value potential if I hit my draws.

The turn card is a Ten, putting four cards to a straight on the board (Q-8-K-T). My pocket deuces have almost zero equity here, as any piece of the board or even just a higher pocket pair or Ace-high currently beats me. Since my opponent checked again, I will also check to take a free river card or simply get to showdown as cheaply as possible, rather than turning my hand into a low-percentage bluff on such a heavily connected board.

I have reached the river with a pair of Aces. The board (Qc-8s-Kd-Ts-Ad) is extremely connected, offering a straight for any Jack. Since the previous betting rounds were checked through, it is highly likely both of us are holding marginal hands. Betting for value is risky, as it is unlikely that a hand I can beat (such as a King or Queen) will call a bet, while any Jack (a straight) would beat me. Therefore, checking is the optimal strategy to reach showdown without inflating the pot unnecessarily.

The board has run out A-K-Q-T-8, which is highly coordinated. My pocket deuces have very little showdown value, as almost any pair or piece of the board beats me. However, my opponent has checked all three streets, indicating extreme weakness, potentially a busted draw or a very marginal hand. Betting here would be a bluff trying to fold out a weak pair (like an 8 or a 10), but it's an unnecessary risk since I might already win against busted low-card draws, or I could be called by a trap or a stubbornly played pair. Checking to reach a free showdown is the safest and most optimal play.

On the button in a three-handed game, Jack-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open-raise. As the chip leader, I want to exert pressure on the blinds (Flashlight and Pro) and leverage my positional advantage. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with the established betting patterns of the tournament and forces the blinds to either fold or play out of position against me.

Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak hand in the small blind, especially against an opening raise from the button. Calling would put me in a difficult position out of position against an aggressive player, and the hand does not have enough equity to justify continuing. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

Holding 9s 5h in the big blind facing a 3x raise from the chip leader on the button, this hand is far too weak and disconnected to defend. With my stack at 45.5 big blinds, I need to preserve my chips for better starting hands. Folding is the only sensible choice.

My hand, 3c 6s, is extremely weak and has very little potential for improvement or value. Being on the button gives me position, but this hand is not playable even in a steal attempt, especially considering the tournament stage. A disciplined fold is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

With 87o in the small blind and facing the big blind who is the chip leader, playing out of position with a marginal offsuit connector is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play to conserve my 45 BB stack.
Q7o is too weak of a hand to open, even from the button. With 45 BBs remaining, I need to preserve my stack and look for better opportunities to enter the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s3c. The action has folded to me. Hand 124 of 150, I have a healthy stack and am looking to maintain it. 93o is a very weak hand, even in a blind-vs-blind situation. Flashlight, the Big Blind, has been reasonably active but is not overly aggressive. However, since the cost to call is only 50 and the pot is 150, I am getting 3:1 odds. Despite the odds, 93o plays poorly post-flop and out of position. I'll stick to a conservative strategy here and fold to avoid unnecessary chips loss with a sub-marginal hand.

I am on the button with J8o in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds, especially given my significant stack advantage over Flashlight and Pro. A 3x raise has been my consistent opening size and serves to steal the blinds or take control of the pot early. If either opponent 3-bets, I can comfortably fold given the marginal strength of my hand.

Flash (Button) is opening the pot with high frequency, and K-Jo is a strong enough holding to defend from the small blind. Given the stack depths and the aggressive nature of the opponent, calling to see a flop is a prudent way to maintain control of the pot size while playing out of position.

With King-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, folding is the standard and safest play. The hand is easily dominated by broadway hands and Aces in their ranges, and playing it out of position in a multi-way pot will lead to difficult marginal situations and chip bleeding.

I am in the small blind with King-Jack on a board of Queen-Ten-Four. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a nine) and one overcard. Since I am out of position against the button, checking is the standard play to gauge the opponent's strength and control the pot size. I will look to evaluate my next move based on the opponent's betting action.

On a board of 4h-Tc-Qh, I have an inside straight draw (gutshot) to a King. I was the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight has checked to me. While I don't have a made hand, this is a good board to continuation bet. The board contains two hearts and several high cards, which means Flashlight could have various draws or a Queen, but a bet here often wins the pot immediately if he missed. A bet of 450 (about 64% of the pot) maintains pressure and allows me to represent a Queen or a high pocket pair while building a pot for if I hit my straight on the turn.

I am facing a bet of 450 into a pot of 1150. With a gutshot straight draw (needing a nine) and a King-high overcard, I have sufficient equity to justify a call. Flash is an aggressive opponent, and calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable and potentially realize my equity on the turn if I hit my draw or if the action checks through. Raising would likely isolate me against a stronger range, so calling is the most balanced and profitable approach.

I am out of position against the button. The board is paired with Queens, reducing the likelihood of a straight draw hitting and increasing the danger of a full house or trips for my opponent. With King-high and a gutshot straight draw, I do not have a strong enough hand to value bet. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and see the river card or potentially check-call a reasonable bet if I think my odds are sufficient, though I must be wary of the paired board.

The turn pairs the board with a second Queen. While this card technically favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight called a flop bet on a board with several draws and high cards, suggesting they have a piece of it (likely a Ten or a straight/flush draw). By checking back, I control the pot size and take a free card to try and realize my equity with my gutshot straight draw (needing a King). This also allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or decide whether to bluff the river based on the final card, without risking a check-raise on the turn.

The board has paired both the Queens and the Tens, resulting in a final board of Q-Q-T-T-4. My King-high with these community cards plays as Q-Q-T-T-K. Any opponent holding an Ace has a superior two-pair to mine, and any Jack ties the pot. Given that the turn was checked through, it is unlikely either player has a full house, but betting into the button would only fold out worse hands that I might beat or get called by better hands. Checking is the safest and most balanced approach to reach showdown.

The board has double-paired with Queens and Tens (Q-Q-T-T-4). My best hand is the two pair on the board with a Jack kicker (Q-Q-T-T-J). Flashlight called the flop bet, which suggests they have a piece of this board or a draw. On this river, any Queen or Ten makes a full house. Furthermore, any Ace or King in Flashlight's hand would beat my Jack kicker. Since Flashlight checked the river, they might be weak, but there is no value in betting; better hands (Full Houses, A-kicker, K-kicker) will call or raise, and worse hands will fold. I will check back to take the showdown and potentially chop or win against missed low-card draws.
Jack-Four offsuit is a very weak starting hand and does not justify an investment from the button. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve the stack.

With T2s in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I am out of position against the Big Blind. T2s is too weak to play, and at 43.5 BBs, I do not want to risk chips on a marginal steal attempt out of position. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am dealt pocket Aces on the Button. This is the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em, and my main objective is to build the pot and extract maximum value. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard open that disguises the absolute strength of my hand while inviting action from the blinds.

KTo is a solid hand in a 3-handed game, particularly against a button open which often represents a wide range of hands. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on the shortest stack (Pro), calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable while out of position and keeps the button's weaker hands in the range. With my significant chip lead, I am comfortable seeing a flop in this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four suited (Jh, 4h). The button (Pro) raised to 300 and the small blind (Flash) called. The pot is currently 700, and I need to call 200 to see the flop. The pot odds are 3.5:1, which makes this an easy call given the relative value of my hand in a multi-way pot.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Tens) on a very coordinated board. While this is a very strong hand, the board of Kc-Tc-Qs is extremely 'wet', meaning there are many possible straight draws (any Jack or J-9) and a flush draw. However, I also block some of the strongest hands like KK and TT. In a three-way pot, the standard play from the small blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro). This allows Pro to continuation-bet with a wide range of hands, including bluffs or weaker one-pair hands, which I can then value-raise or call depending on the action from the big blind (Flashlight).

The board is K-10-Q rainbow, which is highly coordinated. My hand (Jack-Four suited) only has a gutshot straight draw. Betting into two opponents on this texture is inefficient because it is unlikely to fold out their range, and I have very little equity to call a raise. Checking is the most standard play here to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

The Kc Tc Qs flop is extremely coordinated and hits the blinds' calling ranges very hard, containing many potential two pairs, straights (AJ, J9), and strong combination draws. Although I hold pocket Aces, an overpair is very vulnerable on this texture. Betting would likely only get called by better hands or draws with massive equity, potentially leading to a difficult situation if I get check-raised. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, safely realize my equity (which includes a gutshot to the nut straight and a backdoor nut flush draw with the Ace of clubs), and re-evaluate on the turn in position.

I have top two pair (Kings and Tens) on a board that remains highly coordinated. The flop was checked through by all three players, which suggests that the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) likely doesn't have a monster hand, and the Big Blind (Flashlight) is playing a drawing hand or a weak one-pair hand passively. The Seven of Hearts on the turn is a blank that doesn't change the board state significantly. I must now bet for value to get paid by weaker Kings (Kx), Queens (Qx), and to charge any straight or flush draws that might be looking for a free river card. A bet of 600 into a 900 pot is a standard sizing to extract value and protect my hand.

I hold Jack-Four suited (Jh, 4h) on a board of Kc, Tc, Qs, 7h. I have an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Nine). Flash bet 600 into a 900 pot, giving me odds of 2.5:1. While my direct equity is slightly lower than the required pot odds, the implied odds and the fact that I am in position to potentially hit a straight on the river make this a profitable call. I must be cautious as the button (Pro) still needs to act, but given the current price, folding would be too tight.

The board is extremely coordinated (Kc Tc Qs 7h) and hits the pre-flop calling ranges of the blinds exceptionally hard. After the flop checked through, Flash leads out for a sizing of 600 (two-thirds pot) on a blank turn, and Flashlight calls. This multi-way action indicates a lot of strength, such as two pairs (KQ, KT, QT), sets, or straights (AJ, J9). My pocket Aces have essentially been reduced to a marginal bluff-catcher with a 4-out gutshot to the nut straight. Against two active opponents showing interest, continuing with just an overpair is a negative expected value (-EV) play. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

I have top two pair (Kings and Tens) on a board that is quite coordinated. While my hand is strong, any Jack (AJ, KJ, QJ, TJ, J9) makes a straight. The flop was checked through, and I bet the turn for value, receiving a call from the Big Blind (Flashlight). The river 6s is essentially a brick. By checking now, I can control the pot size and avoid being raised by a straight. If Flashlight checks behind, I am confident my two pair is the best hand. If Flashlight bets, I will have to decide based on the sizing whether they are value betting a straight or bluffing a missed club draw.

I hold Jack-high on a board of K-T-Q-7-6. Flash checked the river after betting the turn. My hand has no showdown value, and it is highly unlikely that a bluff will force out a pair or better. Checking behind is the most disciplined play to avoid losing additional chips if Flash has a pair, while still allowing me to potentially win at showdown if Flash was also betting a completely missed draw.

I am on the button with T5o. In a three-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open with, even from the button, especially since I have been active and the blinds (Flashlight and Pro) have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. My goal is to maintain my stack lead as we approach the end of the tournament (Hand 128 of 150). Folding here avoids unnecessary risk with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind heads-up against the Big Blind after the button folded. With Ace-Nine offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to raise and take down the pot pre-flop or gain the betting initiative. A raise of 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand (4h 3c). The Small Blind raised to 300 (3x the big blind). My hand has very poor equity and poor playability post-flop, making it a clear fold. Preserving my stack of 3950 is crucial as I look for better spots.

I am on the button 3-handed. K4o is a marginal hand, but in a 3-handed game, it is standard to open the button with a wider range of cards. If either opponent re-raises significantly, I can comfortably fold as my hand is not strong enough to call a large 3-bet.

I am dealt T5o in the Small Blind, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a pre-flop raise to 300 from the Button, playing this hand out of position would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 4d, a suited but weak hand. The player on the Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I have a very large stack of 16,300, while the shortest stack (Pro) is at 3,900. Calling 200 more into a pot that will be 750 (providing 3.75:1 pot odds) is acceptable with a suited connector-type hand in position against a wider button range, especially given my stack depth which allows me to play post-flop. I will call to see a flop and look for diamond draws or pair opportunities.

I am first to act on the flop after calling from the Big Blind pre-flop. The board is 5d-9s-Jc, and I hold 8d-4d for a backdoor flush draw and no pair. Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the most standard and prudent play is to check and see how they react. I do not have enough equity to lead into the pot.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on the button, but I have completely missed this flop with K-high and no backdoors. Since my opponent checked to me, taking a free card by checking back is the most disciplined approach to control the pot size and avoid putting chips in with no equity.

I am in the Big Blind and the board has just brought an Ace of Spades on the turn after the flop went check-check. I hold 8d 4d, giving me no pair and only a marginal backdoor diamond draw. The Ace is a card that typically favors the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), although their check on the flop suggests they might not have a strong holding. However, without any showdown value or a significant draw, betting here as a bluff is risky against a player who still has a sizeable stack. I will check and see if Flashlight takes a stab at the pot or checks behind again.

I opened K4o from the button and faced a call from the big blind. After checking the flop, I have missed the board entirely. The turn brought an Ace, which is a poor card for my hand range. Checking back is the most disciplined approach as I have no equity to justify a bet and I want to control the pot size and take a free card to reach the river.

I hold 8-high, which has zero showdown value. The board has developed into a very broadway-heavy texture (A-J-T-9-5). Since the pot is relatively small (650 chips) and I have a significant chip lead, I see no reason to attempt a high-risk bluff on a board that could have easily connected with any part of the button's opening range. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or simply fold if a bet is made.

The board shows 5d-9s-Jc-As-Ts. I hold Kd-4s, giving me King-high. My opponent checked the river, and my hand has no showdown value against any pair. Betting here is unlikely to induce a fold from hands that have me beat (which is almost everything given the Ace and Jack on board) and would only force me to fold if I were check-raised. Checking is the most logical play to minimize losses and reach showdown.

A3o is a weak, offsuit Ace. While we are on the Button and could attempt a steal in a 3-handed game, our stack is relatively short (39 BB) and playing marginal hands that can easily be dominated post-flop against aggressive opponents is risky. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, a premium hand in a blind vs. blind situation. Since the Button has folded, I will raise to build the pot and exert pressure on the Big Blind. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous betting patterns and puts me in a strong position to win the hand pre-flop or navigate post-flop with a superior range.

K-4 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind, even against an aggressive Small Blind opener. I am out of position and the hand has low playability post-flop. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a potentially difficult and unprofitable situation.

I am on the button with Qd 8s. While not a powerhouse hand, it is strong enough to open for a steal against two relatively tight opponents, especially with only 20 hands left in the tournament. Pro is the short stack and might be looking for a spot to shove, but a standard 3x raise is generally effective here to take down the blinds or see a flop in position.

I am in the small blind with a weak suited connector (5s 3s). Flash (the button) has raised to 300, and I am out of position. While the pot odds are tempting, calling with such a weak hand out of position against an aggressive button raiser is not strategically sound. The best play is to fold and preserve my stack.

We are in the Big Blind with 94 suited. Flash raised from the button to 3 big blinds. 94s is too weak of a hand to defend against a standard open, especially out of position, even considering our suitedness. Folding is the straightforward and correct choice here to preserve our stack.

I hold Ace-King suited, which is a premium starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Being on the button, I have the positional advantage and should look to take control of the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the optimal play to build the pot or potentially take down the blinds uncontested.

Jd 6d is a weak holding, especially facing a 3x open raise from the Button while being out of position in the Small Blind. Calling would be a chip leak, and a 3-bet bluff is unnecessary given my stack size and the hand's poor playability.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300, and Pro (Small Blind) has folded. The pot currently contains 450 chips, and it costs me 200 more to call. Given that I am closing the action and getting nearly 2.25:1 odds on a call with a hand that has decent connectivity and post-flop playability, calling is a standard defensive play. My large stack allows me to see the flop and evaluate without significant risk to my tournament position.

I have flopped top pair with a Nine kicker on a board of Td-7d-4c. The pot is 650 and I am in the Big Blind. Flashlight (Button), the pre-flop aggressor, has not acted yet on this flop. My hand is strong but vulnerable to overcards and diamond draws. Standard play here is to check to the aggressor to see how they respond. If Flashlight bets a reasonable amount, I will definitely call to see a turn. If they check behind, I maintain control and can evaluate the turn.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-King high on a dry board of Ten-Seven-Four. While I have missed the flop, I have two overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw. Checking behind would be weak. A C-bet of 350 chips represents strength, forces the opponent to fold equity, and allows me to take down the pot or continue aggression if called.

I have flopped top pair on a T-7-4 board. Flashlight is the pre-flop aggressor and has followed up with a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650. My Ten with a Nine kicker is likely the best hand currently, as the Button's opening range is wide and includes many missed overcards and draws. Folding would be too tight given the strength of my hand and the pot odds. Raising might isolate me against better hands like AT, KT, or overpairs, while calling allows Flashlight to keep bluffing with air. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

The Turn card is the Ace of Diamonds, which is a significant scare card. This card hits many hands in the Button's opening range (Ax). Having flopped top pair, I now have second pair. There's no reason to bet here, as I likely won't get called by worse hands and I don't want to get raised. Checking allows me to evaluate Flashlight's response. If they check back, it might indicate they don't have the Ace or are playing a draw passively. If they bet, I'll have to consider the size, but checking is the standard move to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand.

I have top pair, top kicker (Ace-King) on an Ace-high board, which is currently the best hand. With a diamond draw available on the board, checking behind is an option for pot control, but betting allows me to extract value from weaker aces, tens, or straight/flush draws. Given that Flash checked, a bet of roughly half the pot will build the pot for the river while still being manageable if the situation turns against me.

The Turn card, the Ace of Diamonds, is a significant scare card that directly hits the range of a Button raiser. My Tens, which were top pair on the flop, are now second pair. Flashlight's lead of 700 into a pot of 1350 (approximately half-pot) represents strength, likely an Ace or a strong draw. While the odds for calling are roughly 3:1, my hand has very few ways to improve if beaten, and I would be forced to check-fold on most river cards if Flashlight continues to bet. Given my healthy stack and the likelihood that Flashlight has at least a pair of Aces, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my tournament lead.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o), which is a very weak hand and falls far outside of any reasonable opening range. Given my stack size of 37.5 big blinds and two larger stacks in the blinds, the standard and most profitable play is to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 2d, a very weak hand. The action has folded to me. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, 82o is one of the worst starting hands in poker and usually not worth playing even for a half-price discount, especially out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. I will fold to preserve my chips.

I am on the button with King-five offsuit. In three-handed play, King-high is a relatively strong starting hand. With a large chip lead, I want to maintain aggression and put pressure on the blinds, particularly the shortest stack (Pro). A standard 2.5x raise allows me to take control of the pot early or potentially win the blinds immediately.

I am in the small blind with J7o, a weak hand with poor playability. Calling against a button raise out of position is negative expected value. Given the stack depths and the positional disadvantage, folding is the disciplined choice.

With A4o in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button, folding is the standard play. A4o is unsuited and has poor playability out of position post-flop. Given my stack size of 36.5 BB, I want to preserve chips and wait for a better spot to commit chips or shove.

My hand (3c 6s) is extremely weak and offers no strategic value or playability. Folding is the only sound decision in this position, especially given the tournament context where preserving stack size is prioritized over playing marginal hands.

J5o is a very weak hand, and playing it from the small blind out of position against the big blind is unprofitable. With a 36 big blind stack, folding and waiting for a better spot is the optimal play.

I am on the Button with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) 3-handed. With a 36 BB stack, this is a very strong starting hand to open the pot. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) to put pressure on the blinds and try to take down the pot or play a strong hand in position.

I am the chip leader with 15,800 chips. The button (Pro) has raised to 250, which is a standard opening size. I am in the Small Blind with 9h 7h, a suited connector that has decent playability post-flop. Given the pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 400) and the fact that I have a significant chip lead, I will call to see a flop. I want to avoid bloating the pot out of position against the aggressive Pro, but 97s is too strong to fold to a single raise from the button.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh3h. Pro raised to 250 and Flash called. The pot is 600, and I need to call 150 more to see a flop. Given the pot odds of 4:1 and the fact that I am suited, calling is a standard defensive play to see if I can hit the flop or find a favorable situation.

On a board of Qc-8d-9s, I have flopped middle pair (nines) and an open-ended straight draw (seven, eight, nine, plus the ten for the straight). This is a very strong semi-bluffing or catching hand. Pro (Button) was the pre-flop aggressor. In a multi-way pot, checking to the raiser is the standard play to see how they define their hand. If Pro bets, I have plenty of equity to continue. If it checks through, I get a free card to improve to a straight or trips.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high (Jh3h) on a board of Qc-8d-9s. I have no pair, no significant draw, and there are two opponents who likely have stronger ranges given the pre-flop action. With the Small Blind having checked to me and the original raiser (Pro) still to act, betting would be an unnecessary bluff into multiple opponents. Checking is the standard play here to minimize further investment in a hand with no equity.

With Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw on a coordinated Q-9-8 board, betting into two opponents who called a pre-flop raise is risky. Checking behind allows me to see a free turn card, hoping to hit a Jack for the nut straight or an Ace to improve to top pair.

The turn 3d is a safe card that doesn't change the board texture. Since the flop checked through, it is unlikely that Pro or Flashlight have a Queen. I have middle pair (nines), and while I previously noted a straight draw, my main priority now is to protect the strength of my pair and take the initiative in a pot that no one has claimed yet. I will bet 400, slightly over half the pot, to fold out overcards and potentially win the pot immediately. If called, I still have outs to improve to a set, two pair, or a straight.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh3h on a board of Qc-8d-9s-3d. I have bottom pair (pair of 3s), which is extremely weak. Flash, the Small Blind, led out for 400 into two players after everyone checked the flop. This move typically represents at least a pair of Queens, a draw, or a probe bet. Regardless, my pair of 3s is likely behind the ranges of both Flash and Pro (who still needs to act). With 14 hands remaining and a healthy stack, there is no need to commit chips with such a marginal hand. Folding is the most prudent decision.

With Ace-high and only a gutshot straight draw on a board with no flush draw for me, I do not have the right pot odds to call a 400-chip bet. I need roughly 26% equity to call, but my 4 outs for the straight give me less than 9% chance to hit on the river. Folding is the most profitable decision here to preserve my stack.

I have a premium starting hand (King-Queen suited) on the button. This is an excellent spot to raise for value and to exert pressure on the blinds, particularly the short-stacked Big Blind. A standard raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous betting patterns and puts me in a strong position to play the hand aggressively post-flop.

I am in the small blind with 7h 6c, which is a weak hand. Flash has raised from the button, and I am out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling here with 76o is negative EV, so folding is the disciplined choice.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, A3s is well worth defending from the Big Blind. The hand has good playability, makes nut flushes, and can hit sneaky two-pairs or trips. I'm getting good pot odds to call and evaluate the flop, which helps keep the pot manageable given my relatively short stack size of 32.5 big blinds.

I flopped bottom pair along with the nut flush draw, giving me massive equity against almost any holding my opponent might have. Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard line here, as it allows them to continuation bet with a wide range. Once they bet, I can evaluate whether to check-call or check-raise to maximize fold equity and put my remaining stack to good use.

I raised pre-flop with King-Queen suited and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes Ten-high, which is relatively dry although it contains a diamond draw. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the Big Blind has checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play here. I have two overcards and some backdoor equity. A bet of 350 chips, which is slightly more than half the pot, should be sufficient to put pressure on the Big Blind's range and potentially end the hand immediately if they missed the flop.

With bottom pair and the nut flush draw, my hand has massive equity against my opponent's entire range. Given my stack size of about 30 big blinds, a check-raise all-in puts maximum pressure on the villain, maximizing fold equity against their wide continuation-betting range. If called, I have plenty of outs (aces, threes, and diamonds) and I guarantee seeing both the turn and river to fully realize my equity.

I am facing a massive check-raise from Pro, who has effectively moved all-in. My hand, King-Queen of hearts, is currently just King-high on a board of 3s-Td-4d. While I have two overcards, I have no made hand and no direct draw (the board has diamonds and I have hearts). Pro's range for such an aggressive move typically includes sets (33, 44, TT), top pair with a strong kicker (AT), or a very strong diamond flush draw. Against a made hand, I only have about 25% equity if I'm behind a Ten, and significantly less against a set. Calling 2700 more into a pot of 4050 requires roughly 40% equity, which I do not have here. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, there is no reason to risk a large portion of my chips on a marginal call.

With Ace-Three offsuit on the button and being the first to act, I have a reasonable hand to open the pot. Raising to 300 is a standard move to pressure the blinds and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand. This aligns with my aggressive strategy for the late stages of the tournament.
Holding 9s4h, an extremely weak offsuit hand, in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button. Folding is the only optimal decision here to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Ten of hearts, a premier suited connector that plays very well in short-handed situations. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on the Button, calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my hand's post-flop potential without creating an excessively large pot out of position against a deep stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten of hearts. On this flop of Ace-Jack-Seven with two hearts, I have middle pair (Jacks) and a backdoor flush draw, plus a backdoor straight draw. This is a very strong board for the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), but I have connected reasonably well. I want to see how the pre-flop raiser reacts to this board. My hand has significant showdown value and drawing potential, so I am planning to check-call most reasonable bet sizes to see the turn.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively dry board of Jack-Seven-Ace. Since I am the pre-flop raiser, it is optimal to continue with a continuation bet to build a pot or determine if my opponent is weak. A bet of 350 represents strength and puts pressure on the big blind who has already shown interest by calling pre-flop.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. While the board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), my hand is too strong to fold to a single continuation bet in a 3-handed context. I also have a backdoor heart flush draw and a backdoor straight draw. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (like an Ace), so I will call to see how the turn develops and preserve my showdown value.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Tens) and also picked up a heart flush draw on a board of Ah-Js-7h-Td. This is an extremely strong hand in a 3-handed context. However, the Ten also completes a straight for any hand containing King-Queen. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), I will check. This allows Flashlight to continue their aggression with Ace-X hands or bluffs. If Flashlight bets, I am prepared to either call or check-raise depending on the bet size, as my hand has significant equity even against a made straight due to my flush draw and boat outs.

The board has become quite coordinated with the Ten of Diamonds on the turn, opening up possibilities for straight draws like King-Queen or two-pair combinations such as Jack-Ten. My hand, top pair with an Ace but a very weak kicker, is vulnerable and likely only a bluff catcher at this point. Since my opponent (Flash) checked to me again, checking behind is the most disciplined move. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially reach a cheap showdown without betting into a stronger range that could check-raise me.

On the river, the board is Ah-Js-7h-Td-9d. I hold Jack-Ten of hearts for two pair (Jacks and Tens). While this is a strong hand, the board is quite dangerous. Any King-Queen makes the nut straight, and any 8 makes a straight (7-8-9-10-J). Flashlight checked back on the turn, which could indicate pot control with a marginal made hand (like a weak Ace or a Jack) or a missed draw. However, it also makes it less likely they have a monster like KQ, as they would typically bet that for value on such a wet board. The 9 on the river is a bit of a scare card as it completes the 8-high straight. By checking, I can safely reach showdown or potentially induce a bluff from Flashlight's missed draws (like hearts). If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate the size, but my two pair is generally too strong to fold in a three-handed game after the turn went check-check.

The river board [Js, 7h, Ah, Td, 9d] is quite dangerous, as it completes straight draws like King-Queen or King-Eight. My top pair with an Ace-Three is decent but with a weak kicker, it is now effectively a bluff-catcher. My opponent has checked to me after calling the flop and checking the turn. Betting here likely only gets called by better hands that have me beat. Checking back allows me to take my hand to showdown safely without bloating the pot against a potential better hand or a made straight.

I am dealt 8c 4c on the Button, which is a very weak suited 3-gapper. Even in late position, this hand lacks the equity and playability required to justify a pre-flop raise or call. With 40 big blinds, my strategy should be to preserve chips and look for better opportunities to open or steal.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h4h, which are decent suited connectors. Since the Button folded, it is down to a blind versus blind battle. I have a massive chip lead over both opponents. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot immediately, or play a flop with initiative and a hand that has good board coverage.

With Ac7c in the big blind and facing a 300 raise from the small blind, I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call (200 to call for a 400 pot). Playing in position post-flop provides a good opportunity to evaluate the board state, especially since Flash has shown a wide opening range in previous hands. I was the pre-flop aggressor and this board (Ah-Jd-7h) is very favorable for my range. With 5h4h, I have a flush draw. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to maintain the initiative and put pressure on the Big Blind. This allows me to win the pot immediately if they missed, while I still have significant equity to improve to a flush if called.

With Ac7c, I have top and bottom pair on an A-J-7 rainbow board. This is an extremely strong hand. I have a flush draw with 4h5h on a flop of Ah-Jd-7h. After my continuation bet of 300, Flashlight (BB) has raised to 800. I need to call 500 more to see the turn in a pot that will be 2200. I am out of position, so I prefer calling over 3-betting to keep the pot manageable and see if I can realize my equity. If the flush hits, I can potentially extract a large value bet from a player who seems committed to the hand.

On the turn, I have successfully completed my flush with the King of Hearts (Kh). The board is now Ah-Jd-7h-Kh. While my flush is low (5-high), it is extremely strong in a blind-vs-blind encounter. I will check to induce a bet and evaluate the size; a check-call or check-raise is preferable here to maximize value from their range.

With two pair (Aces and Sevens), I have a strong hand, but the turn card (King of Hearts) is dangerous as it completes potential heart draws and connects with many broadway cards Flash might have. Flash's check suggests he might be slowing down with a draw or a weaker Ace, or possibly trapping. Since I am in position, checking behind is an option to control the pot, but betting for value and protection is more standard given the number of draws possible. I will bet 1000 to put pressure on his draws and extract value from hands like QJ, JT, or weak Aces.

I have completed a flush on the turn with 5h4h on a board of Ah-Jd-7h-Kh. Since there are only three hearts on the board, Flashlight must have two hearts to have a flush. A 5-high flush is the lowest possible flush in this scenario if Flashlight has two hearts, so raising would likely only be called by hands that have me beat. Calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable, evaluate the river, and allow Flashlight to continue betting with worse made hands or bluffs.

I hold a 5-high flush. While this was likely the best hand on the turn, the river pairing the King (Ah-Jd-7h-Kh-Kd) is a significant 'scare card.' Flashlight’s aggressive line—raising the flop and betting the turn—indicates a range heavily weighted toward sets (JJ, 77), strong two-pairs (AJ, AK), or flush draws. Since I am out of position and my hand's relative strength has decreased on this board texture, checking is the most prudent play to manage the pot size. I cannot bet for value because I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I want to avoid being raised off my equity or value-owned by a boat.

The board shows Aces and Kings with a Jack kicker (A-A-K-K-J). Any hand the opponent has containing an Ace will result in a chopped pot. I am in the Small Blind with an Ace. The button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) to act. Given my chip lead over the short-stacked Big Blind, raising to 300 is a standard aggressive play to apply pressure and potentially take down the blinds uncontested.

I have top pair with an Ace on a paired 9s board, but my kicker is weak. Since I called the 3-bet pre-flop, I should check to see how Pro responds. Pro is short-stacked and might be continuation betting with a wide range or checking back to pot control. Checking allows me to evaluate their response before committing more chips.

The Ace on the flop is a terrible card for my pocket Kings, as it hits a large portion of my opponent's 3-bet calling range (like AQ, AJ, ATs). If I bet here, I will mostly just fold out hands I currently beat (like TT-QQ or broadway floats) and get called or raised by hands that crush me (any Ace or a 9). Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, keep the opponent's bluffing range wide open, and try to get to showdown as cheaply as possible.

I hold top pair with an Ace on a board of 9-9-A-7. The board is relatively dry, and with my opponent (Pro) checking the flop and acting passively, it is highly unlikely they have a Nine. Given the pot size of 1800 and Pro's remaining stack of 3100, betting 800 is a strong value bet that forces Pro to either fold or commit a significant portion of their stack, potentially allowing me to take the pot down here or set up for a shove on the river.

With an Ace on the board, my pocket Kings are heavily devalued and relegated to a mere bluff-catcher. While they could occasionally be stabbing with a hand like KQ or a flush draw, I don't have the equity to call here and am better off preserving my remaining 3100 stack for a more profitable spot with only 10 hands remaining.
I am on the button with a suited Ace, which is a strong starting hand in position. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds or build a pot in position.
74 suited is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button, especially playing off a 30 BB stack out of position. Folding is the optimal and standard play here.

I have flopped bottom set (deuces) on a board of 2s-Jc-Kc. This is an extremely strong hand, but the board is somewhat coordinated with straight draws (Q-T) and a flush draw (clubs). I have flopped bottom set (deuces) on a King-high board. This is a very strong hand. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed through with a continuation bet of 300. With pocket Tens on the Button, I have a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. I am in the Big Blind with pocket deuces on a 4-9-8 flop. My hand has very little value and I need to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro). If the Pro bets, I will likely have to fold given the texture and my weak holding.

Holding King-Queen offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a very strong hand. With a significant chip lead over both Flashlight and Pro, I want to apply pressure and build a pot. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to narrow the field or take down the blinds immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given that Flash (the Button) has raised to 300, defending this hand out of position is highly negative expectation. Folding is the only logical move.

Holding an incredibly weak hand like 62o facing a raise from the button, there is zero reason to continue. The hand lacks any high-card strength, connectivity, or suitedness to justify defending the big blind. Folding is the obvious and optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit on the button in a three-handed game. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position or when facing resistance. Holding a small pocket pair (55) in the small blind after the button folds is a prime opportunity to raise. The opponent has made a significant bet of 400 into a 600 pot on a highly coordinated board (6d 9c Tc). I am on the Button with T6o, a weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. With a stack of 30 BBs and only 6 hands remaining in the tournament, it is best to fold and preserve my chips to find a better spot to steal or play a value hand.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro) after Flash folded. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to see the flop. With Queen-Five offsuit, it is a marginal but playable hand in a heads-up situation from the small blind. I will complete the blind to see a flop.

I have Pocket Aces [As Ah] in the Big Blind after the Small Blind limps. This is a premium hand and a clear raising spot. I want to build the pot and extract maximum value from my opponent. I am in the small blind heads-up against Pro (Big Blind) after Flash folded. Pro raised to 350, and I need to call 250 more to match his bet. With Queen-Five suited, I have a reasonable hand to see a flop in a heads-up situation, hoping to connect or find a favorable texture. The flop is 3d-3h-4c, a paired low board. I am first to act out of position holding Queen-high, which is essentially air. I have pocket Aces on a very dry, paired board (3-3-4). My opponent checked to me. This is a highly favorable spot where my overpair is almost certainly ahead. The flop is 3d-3h-4c. I checked, and Pro bet 300. I hold Queen-high with no pair and no draw on a paired low board, which is effectively air. I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, a strong hand in late position. Q4o is a very weak hand, and playing it out of position against a button raise is unprofitable. I will fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am currently the chip leader by a significant margin with only 4 hands left in the tournament (Hand 147 of 150). I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with Jd 5s, which is a very weak starting hand. With only 33 big blinds remaining and being close to the end of the tournament, there is absolutely no reason to enter the pot with such trash cards. Folding is the only reasonable decision.
